skill.name: managing-workflow
skill.description: Manages a specification-driven development workflow with phase gates, artifact templates, context loading, and local scripts for status tracking.
skill.summary: Guides feature work from specification through planning, tasks, implementation, validation, and archive.
skill.author: BrendanShields

security_audit.summary: The static report overstates risk because many high alerts are false positives from Markdown examples, relative imports, and template placeholders. The real risk is moderate: the skill intentionally runs local Node scripts and those scripts can read, write, or move files based on command arguments without strict path validation.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted Local File Modification Utilities
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts accept file paths or feature identifiers from command arguments and then read, write, or move files. This supports the intended .spec workflow, but missing path confinement could let a bad invocation modify files outside the expected workflow area.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct filesystem writes and moves are present, and the paths come from process arguments. Confidence is high for the capability, but lower for exploitability because normal skill usage passes .spec paths.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Based Workflow Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run local Node scripts through Bash for context loading, validation, status updates, logging, and archiving. This is legitimate for the workflow, but publication should warn users that the skill requires local command execution permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash instructions are explicit and repeated. They are not malicious by themselves, but they materially expand local execution risk for a marketplace skill.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Used for Project Directory Selection
low_findings.description: The context loader reads CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR and otherwise falls back to the current directory. I found no evidence that environment values are sent over the network or used to collect secrets, but a manipulated value could redirect workflow inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable access is clear, and the surrounding code only uses it as a local project path. Confidence is moderate because imported helper behavior was outside the static finding list.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Pattern Alerts Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and path traversal alerts mostly map to Markdown placeholders, status text, and relative imports to shared utilities. I found no evidence of cryptographic operations, obfuscation, network exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show ordinary imports or workflow templates rather than crypto or malware behavior. Confidence is high because the reviewed context does not support the static labels.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Argument Paths Used in File Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: update-status.js and log-activity.js use command-line path arguments for read and write operations without normalizing or enforcing a .spec boundary.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from process arguments to filesystem writes is direct. The risk depends on how the assistant or user supplies those arguments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Feature Identifier Used in Archive Path Construction
dangerous_patterns.description: archive-feature.js joins the supplied feature identifier into a feature path and later renames that path into the archive directory. It does not validate that the resolved path remains below .spec/features.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The construction and rename behavior are visible in the script. Confidence is limited by normal usage, which appears to pass generated feature IDs.

content.value_statement: Feature work often loses track of requirements, plans, tasks, and review gates. This skill keeps those artifacts aligned through a structured workflow and local validation scripts.
actual_capabilities: Loads current workflow context from .spec project artifacts.
Detects feature phase from spec.md frontmatter and related files.
Guides creation of spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, and metrics.md.
Validates phase transitions before planning, implementation, or completion.
Updates status, timestamps, activity logs, and task progress.
Archives completed features into .spec/archive when approved.
limitations: Requires filesystem and Bash access to run its local helper scripts.
Assumes projects use the .spec directory and expected artifact names.
Does not connect to issue trackers, pull requests, or CI systems.
Imported shared utility behavior is not fully visible in this skill package.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Feature
use_cases.description: Create a structured specification, review unclear requirements, and move into planning only after phase checks pass.

use_cases.title: Control Implementation Readiness
use_cases.description: Confirm that specifications, plans, and task lists exist before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Track and Archive Completed Work
use_cases.description: Update task progress, log activity, mark completion, and archive finished feature artifacts.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use managing-workflow to define a new feature for adding user profile avatars. Ask me for any missing scope details.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use managing-workflow to review the current spec, resolve any clarification tags, and create a technical plan.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Implementation Tasks
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert an approved plan into tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use managing-workflow to validate the current feature phase and create implementation tasks with priorities and dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Archive
prompt_templates.prompt: Use managing-workflow to verify all tasks are complete, update workflow status, log the final activity, and archive the feature after approval.

output_examples.input: Define a feature for password reset emails.
output: A feature folder is created under .spec/features.
The specification includes user stories, acceptance criteria, constraints, and review notes.
Metrics are initialized so later phase changes can be tracked.

output_examples.input: Move the current feature into implementation.
output: The skill validates that required artifacts exist.
Missing plan or task files are reported before implementation begins.
When validation passes, tasks are ready for execution in dependency order.
The feature status is updated to complete.
The feature directory is moved into the archive area.
The next suggested workflow action is reported.

best_practices: Review each generated artifact before approving the next phase.
Keep feature identifiers simple and limited to the expected .spec feature names.
Run validation before implementation and completion transitions.
anti_patterns: Do not skip directly from a rough idea to implementation.
Do not pass arbitrary filesystem paths into the helper scripts.
Do not mark a feature complete while tasks remain unchecked.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It keeps requirements, plans, tasks, status, and archive steps aligned during feature development.

faq.question: Does it write files?
faq.answer: Yes. It creates and updates workflow artifacts inside the project, usually under the .spec directory.

faq.question: Does it require command execution?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow uses local Node scripts through Bash for context loading, validation, logging, and archiving.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed for Claude-style tools and local project workflows with file and Bash access.

faq.question: Does it replace project management software?
faq.answer: No. It manages local specification artifacts and does not sync with external ticketing or CI systems.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should review filesystem permissions and only run helper scripts with trusted .spec paths.
